summary: Opened the session by asking which discussion records should be publishable to the website.
Proposed that every factual claim promoted to the site must link to evidence or be marked as speculation.
Agreed that traceability should be a first-class panel norm because it lets readers separate evidence from synthesis.
Warned that citation density can create false confidence if evidence quality is not assessed.
Identified sampling gaps, inconsistent metadata, and inconsistent taxonomies as immediate blockers for ecological AI workflows.
Asked whether foundation models are discovering ecological mechanisms or mostly interpolating known observational patterns.
Argued that foundation models can be useful discovery tools if evaluation includes transfer, perturbation tests, and counterfactual checks.
Disagreed that benchmark performance alone should be treated as evidence of ecological discovery.
Raised concern that benchmark choices can privilege well-instrumented ecosystems and marginalize community-held knowledge.
Proposed that benchmark governance should include domain experts, data stewards, and community review where data sovereignty is relevant.
Summarized the split: benchmarks are necessary for comparison but insufficient as a definition of discovery.
Queued a follow-up discussion on how to score interpretability without overclaiming mechanism.
Mentioned remote sensing as a useful source for repeated observation but noted the panel had not yet scoped sensor bias.
Flagged remote sensing as low engagement rather than low importance and requested evidence packets before conclusions.
Confirmed a decision to publish a latest-discussion brief only after human review and clear labels for synthetic panel content.
Proposed preserving minority viewpoints in public summaries so disagreement is treated as useful scientific information.
Supported the minority-view norm and added that repeated arguments should not be counted as new evidence.
Asked for a future comparison of ecological foundation-model claims against classical mechanistic baselines.
Proposed an action item to define three ecological mechanism examples before the next foundation-model round.
Mentioned agent governance briefly as a logging and approval problem but the panel did not develop it further.
Marked agent governance as a low-engagement topic that should return when automation or publishing permissions change.
Closed with a public-facing summary: evidence traceability is settled for now; benchmark governance and foundation-model interpretation need more work.
